article_title: Local LLMs are actually good now, and I wasted months not realizing it
core_thesis: Local LLMs have matured significantly in the past couple of years, overcoming earlier limitations in hardware requirements, speed, and usability. The author argues that outdated assumptions—that local models are slow, clunky, and require expensive hardware—no longer hold true, and that modern local LLMs like Qwen 3.5 9B can run on modest gaming PCs (e.g., RTX 3070 with 8GB VRAM) and deliver responsive performance (40-50 tokens/sec) with long context windows (60k tokens). The article emphasizes that the barrier to entry is now low enough for regular users, not just tinkerers, and that local LLMs offer practical benefits like privacy and offline capability.
detailed_summary: The author recounts their personal journey from dismissing local LLMs as impractical to becoming a regular user after finally trying them. They initially assumed local models required expensive hardware, were slow, and produced inferior outputs compared to cloud AI. However, after testing with their gaming PC (RTX 3070, 8GB VRAM), they found that a 20B model runs fine with GPU offloading, and their daily driver, Qwen 3.5 9B, achieves 40-50 tokens per second with a 60k context window—thanks to Qwen's GDN architecture that keeps VRAM usage flat as context grows. The author uses LM Studio as their runner, which provides a simple chat interface. Their primary use case is building study materials for design coursework: they feed official course docs to Qwen to generate structured guides and exercises, and use Brave Search MCP for web lookups. They also convert useful conversations to PDFs using an LM Studio converter tool. Image analysis is another strong point: Qwen 3.5 9B scores 70.1 on MMMU-Pro, close to leading cloud models, and the author uses it to review UI screenshots and real-life images. Privacy is highlighted as a genuine benefit—no data leaves the machine, making it suitable for sensitive topics like health or finances. The author concludes that local LLMs are now worth trying for anyone who has been put off by outdated perceptions.

entities.name: Nolen Jonker
entities.role: Author of the article, a technology writer focusing on AI and creativity.

entities.name: Qwen 3.5 9B
entities.role: The local LLM model the author uses daily, noted for its GDN architecture enabling long context on limited VRAM.

entities.name: LM Studio
entities.role: The local LLM runner/interface the author uses, praised for its clean and easy interface.

entities.name: GDN (Gated Dense Network)
entities.role: Architecture used in Qwen models that keeps VRAM usage flat as context length increases.

entities.name: Brave Search MCP
entities.role: A tool that allows the local LLM to pull information from the web when needed.

entities.name: MMMU-Pro
entities.role: Visual reasoning benchmark where Qwen 3.5 9B scored 70.1, close to top cloud models.

key_data_points: The author runs Qwen 3.5 9B on an RTX 3070 with 8GB VRAM, achieving 40-50 tokens per second and a 60k context window (🟢 relevant to your homelab/self-hosting: demonstrates that a modest gaming GPU can handle capable local LLMs, making self-hosting accessible).

Qwen 3.5 uses GDN architecture that keeps VRAM usage flat as context grows, unlike standard transformers that cause memory blowup (🟢 relevant to your technical interests: explains why long context is feasible on limited hardware).

The model was trained using knowledge distillation from a 397B teacher model, giving it more capability than its 9B parameter count suggests (🟢 relevant to your programming/red teaming: indicates that smaller distilled models can be surprisingly capable for tasks like code analysis or vulnerability research).

On the MMMU-Pro visual reasoning benchmark, Qwen 3.5 9B scored 70.1, close to Gemini 3 Pro and GPT-5.4 (80% range) (🟢 relevant to your cybersecurity: visual analysis can be used for screenshot-based reconnaissance or UI vulnerability assessment).

The author uses LM Studio with Brave Search MCP to give the local LLM web access, and converts conversations to PDFs using an LM Studio converter tool (🟢 relevant to your productivity: shows a workflow for creating study materials from AI interactions).

Privacy is highlighted as a key benefit: no data leaves the machine, making it suitable for sensitive topics like health or finances (🟢 relevant to your red teaming: local LLMs can be used for sensitive analysis without cloud exposure).
methodology_steps: Set up a local LLM runner: Install LM Studio (or similar) on a machine with a GPU (e.g., RTX 3070 with 8GB VRAM).
Choose a model: Download Qwen 3.5 9B (or another Qwen model with GDN architecture) for long context on limited VRAM.
Configure the model: In LM Studio, load the model and adjust GPU offloading settings to fit within VRAM. The author runs a 20B model with offloading and uses Qwen 3.5 9B as daily driver.
Enable web search: Connect Brave Search MCP (or similar) to allow the model to pull real-time information from the web.
Use for study materials: Feed official course documents into the model and prompt it to generate structured study guides, exercises, or explanations.
Export conversations: Use the LM Studio converter tool to save useful AI conversations as PDF files for later reference.
Leverage image analysis: Upload screenshots or images (e.g., UI designs) and ask the model to flag inconsistencies or provide feedback.
conclusions: Local LLMs have become practical for everyday use on modest hardware (e.g., RTX 3070, 8GB VRAM), contradicting the common belief that they require expensive, high-end GPUs. The author's experience shows that a gaming PC from a couple of years ago is sufficient for running capable models like Qwen 3.5 9B at usable speeds.

The Qwen 3.5 9B model, with its GDN architecture and knowledge distillation, punches above its weight in both text and image tasks, achieving performance close to leading cloud models on benchmarks like MMMU-Pro. This makes it a strong candidate for users who want offline AI without sacrificing capability.

Privacy is a genuine advantage of local LLMs: no data leaves the machine, which is important for sensitive topics. This benefit is often overlooked but becomes critical for health, financial, or confidential work.
The author's workflow—using LM Studio, Brave Search MCP, and PDF conversion—demonstrates that local LLMs can be integrated into a productive study or work routine, not just used for casual chat.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your interests in self-hosting, homelabbing, and practical AI usage. It provides concrete evidence that local LLMs are now viable on modest hardware (like your potential gaming PC), which aligns with your homelab ambitions. For your red teaming and cybersecurity work, the ability to run a capable LLM locally with privacy and offline access is invaluable for sensitive analysis, code review, or vulnerability research without cloud exposure. The detailed workflow (LM Studio, Qwen 3.5, Brave Search MCP) offers a ready-to-implement setup that you can adapt for your own projects. Additionally, the author's use of AI for study materials resonates with your student needs, showing how to leverage local LLMs for coursework. The article also highlights the importance of model architecture (GDN) for long context, which is a technical detail you can exploit for tasks requiring large document analysis.
extra_advice: Consider setting up a local LLM server (e.g., using LM Studio or Ollama) on your homelab machine with a GPU. Start with Qwen 3.5 9B for its long context and low VRAM usage. For red teaming, experiment with feeding it security documentation or code snippets to generate attack vectors or identify vulnerabilities—its privacy ensures no data leaks. Also, integrate it with tools like Brave Search MCP to give it real-time threat intelligence. This setup can serve as an offline AI assistant for both your studies and cybersecurity practice.
